name: Leprechaun
description: Leprechauns are solitary fairy-like beings in Irish folklore, often depicted as diminutive, mischievous cobblers. They are said to be the guardians of hidden treasures, often a pot of gold at the end of the rainbow. Known for their cunning and trickery, leprechauns are notoriously difficult to catch. If captured, they may grant wishes or reveal the location of their treasure in exchange for their freedom, but they are masters of deception and misdirection, making it difficult to outsmart them. They are typically described as being old men, often wearing green coats and hats, and possessing a love for practical jokes and riddles. While generally harmless, they can be fiercely protective of their gold and will use their magic to defend it. Leprechauns are a prominent symbol of Irish culture and are often associated with good luck and fortune, though interacting with them can be fraught with peril due to their unpredictable nature and penchant for trickery. Their origins may stem from earlier Irish spirits associated with the land and its hidden riches.
subtitle: Mischievous cobblers with hidden gold.
appearance: Leprechauns are typically depicted as small, wizened men, standing around two to three feet tall. They often wear a green or red coat, buckled shoes, and a tall, pointed hat. They usually have a beard and a mischievous glint in their eyes. Some depictions show them with a leather apron and tools, reflecting their cobbling profession.
habitat: Leprechauns are said to inhabit remote, rural areas of Ireland, such as forests, hills, and underground caves. They are often found near fairy rings or other places associated with magic and folklore.
behavior: Leprechauns are solitary and secretive creatures, preferring to avoid contact with humans. They are known for their trickery and love of practical jokes, often using their magic to mislead or confuse those who try to find their treasure. They are fiercely protective of their gold and will go to great lengths to defend it. While not inherently malicious, they are unpredictable and can be dangerous if provoked.

keyMyths.title: The Pot of Gold
keyMyths.description: The most famous myth associated with leprechauns is the legend of the pot of gold at the end of the rainbow. Leprechauns are said to guard this treasure, which can be obtained by capturing one and forcing them to reveal its location. However, leprechauns are masters of deception and often trick those who seek their gold.

sources.author: W.B. Yeats
sources.work: Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry
sources.description: A collection of Irish folklore, including stories about leprechauns.
sources.text: A collection of Irish folklore, including stories about leprechauns.

sources.author: Patricia Lysaght
sources.work: The Banshee: The Irish Death Messenger
sources.description: This work, while primarily about Banshees, provides context on the broader Irish fairy tradition and the place of leprechauns within it.
sources.text: This work, while primarily about Banshees, provides context on the broader Irish fairy tradition and the place of leprechauns within it.
